page: Vultr vx1-m-192c-1536g-11520s is an x86_64 architecture server within the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features an AMD processor configured with 192 virtual CPUs and 192 physical cores, operating with a single thread per core. The system is equipped with 1430.0 GB of system memory, yielding approximately 7.45 GB of RAM per core.

This server configuration includes 10720 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. It does not feature any graphics processing units. For connectivity, the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

A key architectural characteristic of this server is its shared CPU allocation model. While it offers a massive core count and substantial memory capacity, the shared CPU resource model represents a tradeoff compared to dedicated instances, making it a cost-efficient option for workloads requiring high memory and storage density without requiring sustained, exclusive CPU performance.

Due to its high memory capacity and large local NVMe storage, this instance is suited for memory-intensive applications, large-scale databases, caching layers, and data processing tasks that benefit from high storage capacity and memory density under a shared-resource model.
description: Vultr vx1-m-192c-1536g-11520s is an x86_64 server belonging to the VX1 Cloud Compute family, powered by an AMD processor. It features 192 virtual CPUs and 192 physical cores with a single thread per core, operating under a shared CPU allocation model. The hardware configuration includes 1430.0 GB of system memory, providing 7.45 GB of RAM per core, alongside 10720 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. No GPU resources are included. This server offers a high-density resource profile, balancing massive memory and storage capacities. The shared CPU allocation presents a tradeoff, making the instance a cost-efficient option for workloads that require substantial memory and storage but do not demand dedicated, continuous CPU performance. It is suited for large-scale databases, memory-intensive data processing, and caching workloads.
